retrieved_docs.content: Pain Infections Hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation Secondary infections

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- History and Physical -- Fixed Drug Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: FDE  is a cutaneous drug reaction that typically presents as a solitary, well-demarcated, round or oval dusky red macular lesion, most commonly on the lips, genitalia, perianal area, hands, and feet. The lesions may progress to form vesicles or blisters. Lesions usually appear within 8 hours of drug exposure, although a delay of up to 2 weeks can occur. Upon reexposure to the drug, the lesion recurs at the same site. Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation is often observed following the resolution of the lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Differential Diagnosis -- Fixed Drug Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: Bullous pemphigoid Erythema multiforme Large plaque parapsoriasis [65] Autoimmune progesterone dermatitis Aphthous stomatitis [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Evaluation -- Fixed Drug Eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of FDE is clinical, based on the characteristic features of the lesions, a history of drug intake hours to days before lesion development, and the recurrence of lesions at the same site upon reexposure to the same drug. A skin biopsy should be considered if the diagnosis is unclear or if systemic or unusual symptoms are present. If the offending drug is not identified, patients may undergo an oral provocation test or patch testing. However, the sensitivity of patch testing is low, making oral provocation the preferred method. [57] Clinicians should avoid performing provocation testing in patients with generalized reactions of FDE.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Adverse Drug Reaction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fixed pigmented erythema (FPE), also known as a fixed drug eruption, occurs 24 hours to a few days after administration of the offending medication. The classic form is characterized by a single or multiple round to oval-shaped, red to brown macules. These lesions have the potential to evolve into plaques with vesicles or blistering.

retrieved_docs.title: Symmetrical Drug-Related Intertriginous and Flexural Exanthema -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: SDRIFE can also mimic other specific drug reactions such as acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP), drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS), and fixed drug eruption (FDE). [4] [9] [16] However, AGEP and DRESS characteristically demonstrate a widespread rash with accompanying systemic symptoms. FDE can be easily differentiated from SDRIFE as it characteristically presents asymmetrical acral, genital, and mucosal patches or plaques, usually round or oval, with residual hyperpigmentation. [13] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs are most frequently implicated in AGEP, as immune-mediated reactions to drugs are more consistent and reproducible than responses to other reported triggers. Early recognition and withdrawal of the causative drug remain the most effective strategies to hasten resolution and prevent complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Evaluation -- Maculopapular Drug Eruptions
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of maculopapular drug eruptions is primarily clinical and is based on the patient's history and physical examination. A skin biopsy does not reliably differentiate these eruptions from rashes associated with viral exanthems and is thus not helpful in confirming the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Symmetrical Drug-Related Intertriginous and Flexural Exanthema -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The latency of the onset of this condition varies from a few hours to a few days after the causative drug is removed. The basis of diagnosis is mainly on the highly characteristic and stereotypic appearance of the rash. The involvement of the gluteal and intertriginous areas and the symmetry of lesions are key diagnostic features. [9] The diagnostic criteria for symmetrical drug-related intertriginous and flexural exanthema are exposure to a systemically administered drug with the exclusion of contact allergens; characteristic well-demarcated erythema involving the gluteal, perianal, inguinal, or perigenital area; involvement of at least one intertriginous area; symmetry of the affected areas; and the absence of systemic symptoms. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Symmetrical Drug-Related Intertriginous and Flexural Exanthema -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Real pathogenetic mechanisms of symmetrical drug-related intertriginous and flexural exanthema are still unclear. Still, it has been suspected to develop as a result of a type IV delayed hypersensitivity immune response. [11] This notion was supported by immunohistochemical evidence for CD4+ T cell infiltration and the increased endothelial and keratinocyte expression of CD26P-selectin, which recruits type 1 helper T cells to the sites of inflammation. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Symmetrical Drug-Related Intertriginous and Flexural Exanthema -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis relies mostly on clinical presentation, history, and exclusion of other causes for a rash. Laboratory investigations are performed to exclude systemic involvement (such as cytopenia and hepatic or renal involvement) but are otherwise not necessary for the diagnosis of SDRIFE. [9] Patch tests, lymphocyte transformation tests, and drug provocation tests (DPT) can be useful for diagnosis, but their outcomes are highly variable.

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- History and Physical -- Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: The latency phase of DIHS/DRESS ranges from 2 to 8 weeks. Symptoms often begin with nonspecific prodromal signs such as fever, malaise, and lymphadenopathy. The classic rash associated with DIHS/DRESS is a profoundly erythematous, maculopapular, symmetric eruption on the trunk and extremities, often coalescing to cover more than 50% of the body's surface. Nearly 70% of patients exhibit significant facial edema, a key diagnostic clue, while mild mucosal involvement occurs in approximately 50% of cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Symmetrical Drug-Related Intertriginous and Flexural Exanthema -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to some authors, SDRIFE likely involves a type IVa reaction involving CD4+ Th1 cells and macrophages and a type IVc reaction with cytotoxic CD4 and CD8 T cells. [12] Nevertheless, this does not explain the occurrence of SDRIFE after the first exposure to a given drug without prior sensitization. [3]

retrieved_docs.content: Fenoprofen and butenafine are both contraindicated in the presence of hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Use of fenoprofen is not recommended for patients who have a hypersensitivity reaction, and similarly, butenafine should be avoided in individuals with hypersensitivity disease. Recognizing hypersensitivity as a contraindicating factor for these medications is essential for safe prescribing and patient care.

Butenafine, tolnaftate and fenoprofen are contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Because each drug is associated with an increased risk of allergic reactions in patients who are hypersensitive, they should be avoided in such patients to prevent adverse immune‑mediated effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض (low white‑blood‑cell count). In patients with قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض, a notable manifestation is عدم تحمل بروتين اللايسين (lysine protein intolerance). Moreover, قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض is classified as a member of متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ), the drug‑reaction‑with‑eosinophilia‑and‑systemic‑symptoms syndrome. This interconnection highlights that leukopenia can present with specific protein intolerance and is recognized within a broader drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome, informing clinicians about potential diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1360
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib mesylate, Etodolac, and Sodium selenate are each contraindicated in patients who have فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). Because a known hypersensitivity reaction to medications can lead to severe adverse effects, these drugs should be avoided in individuals with such a history. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians when evaluating a patient’s allergy profile before initiating therapy with any of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: A positive HIV test result (فحص فيروس نقص المناعة البشرية إيجابي) is clinically associated with leukopenia (قِلَّةُ الكُرَيَّاتِ البِيْض). Leukopenia is also classified as a member of the drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms syndrome (متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ)). This connection indicates that a low white‑blood‑cell count can arise both from HIV infection and as a component of severe drug‑induced hypersensitivity reactions, emphasizing the need for careful monitoring of white‑blood‑cell levels in patients with HIV or those exposed to medications that may trigger this syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Allergic reaction to penicillin is identified as a specific type of adverse effect within the broader category of “e codes: adverse effects of medical drugs.” Penicillin G, a commonly used β‑lactam antibiotic, is included in this category because it can precipitate such drug‑related adverse events. Consequently, penicillin G‑associated allergic reactions exemplify the narrower relationship between individual drug allergies and the general classification of medical drug adverse effects. Recognizing this link aids clinicians in coding, monitoring, and managing drug safety for patients receiving penicillin G.
